title: The Silent Bug Audit: Catch Type Errors Before They Poison Your Analysis

sections.title: Your Last Analysis — Type Audit
prompt: Pick a recent script or notebook. List 3 variables you used. For each, write: (a) what type you assumed it was, (b) what type() actually returns.
Did any column arrive as strings when you expected numbers? How did you find out — error, wrong output, or never?
Write one line where a silent concatenation bug could have hit your work.

sections.title: Lists vs Dicts — What Are You Actually Storing?
prompt: You have employee records with name, salary, department. Would you use a list of dicts or a flat list of salaries? Write your reasoning in one sentence.
Write a list comprehension that filters salaries above your own threshold (pick a number). Don't look it up — write it from memory first, then verify.
When does a list of dicts become the wrong tool? Name one scenario where you'd switch to something else.

sections.title: Function Refactor — Your Duplicated Code
prompt: Identify one block of code you've copy-pasted across notebooks or scripts. Write the block here (or describe it in plain English).
Rewrite it as a named function with at least one parameter. Add a default argument where it makes sense.
What would break if someone passed the wrong type into your function? How would you catch it?

sections.title: The Four Primitives — Intention Check
prompt: For each type below, write one real example from your own data work: int / float / str / bool
Where in your code have you mixed int and float without thinking? What was the result?
Write the conversion that fixes this: age_column = ['28', '35', '42']. You need to sum it.

sections.title: Build Your Personal Type-Safety Checklist
prompt: List 3 columns in datasets you work with regularly. What type should each be? What type does Python often load them as?
Write a 3-step pre-analysis check you'll run before touching any new CSV.

After Tutorial 1's average calculation: rewrite it as a function called calculate_average(). Then add a second function is_outlier(value, data, threshold=2.0) that returns True/False. Paste what you built.

cta: Run type() on every variable in your last notebook. Fix one silent type mismatch you find. Share what the wrong value was and what it should have been.
